content.full_text:
September 27, 1994

Franz Schneider
1044 Monterey Street
Hollister, CA 95023

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-94-283

Dear Mr. Schneider:

We write in response to your August 23, 1994 request for
advice regarding a possible conflict of interest under the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Your question, in part, may
implicate other laws governing the conduct of public officials.
Our jurisdiction is limited to the Act, and we do not offer advice
regarding other laws regulating the conduct of public officials.
We suggest you seek additional guidance from your city attorney.

QUESTION

Does a city planning commissioner have a conflict of interest
under the Act with regard to decisions concerning residential or
nonresidential development by virtue of his employment as a
teacher with the local school district?

CONCLUSION

No. Your position as a school teacher does not give rise to
a conflict of interest under the Act which would prohibit your
participation in decisions by the planning commission.

FACTS

You are a planning commissioner for the City of Hollister.
You are also employed as a school teacher in the Hollister School
District. Decisions concerning development within the city are
expected to have a considerable impact on the school district.

_______________________________

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

File No. I-94-283
Page 2


ANALYSIS

     The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act prohibit a
public official from participating in, or otherwise attempting to
influence a governmental decision which may have a reasonably
foreseeable and material effect on the public official or the
official's financial interest. (Section 87100; Section 87103.) A
planning commissioner is a public official under the Act.
(Section 82048.) A financial interest includes, among other
things, any source of income to the official aggregating two
hundred fifty dollars or more in value received or promised to the
official within twelve months prior to the decision. (Section
87103.)

     Under Section 82030(b), salary and reimbursement for expenses
received from a state, local, or federal government agency are
excluded from the definition of "income." As the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Act prohibit only financial conflicts
of interest, and as your salary as a teacher is not deemed to be a
financial interest under the Act, no conflict exists based on your
dual employment as a teacher and a planning commissioner.

     We trust that this letter fully addresses your request. If
you should have additional questions, please contact the
undersigned at (916) 322-5901.


                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By:  Daniel E. Muallem
                                             Counsel, Legal Division

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does a city planning commissioner have a conflict of interest
under the Act with regard to decisions concerning residential or
nonresidential development by virtue of his employment as a
teacher with the local school district?

CONCLUSION: No.
Your position as a school teacher does not give rise to
a conflict of interest under the Act which would prohibit your
participation in decisions by the planning commission.
